extracted_rules.resource: Right to live under ancestral laws
proof: [§1] The descendants of Pamphylus, and, truly, of the Heracleidae also, dwelling beneath the cliffs of Taÿgetus, are willing to abide forever as Dorians under the ordinances of Aegimius.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific ethnic/lineage group (Dorians/Heracleidae) and their protected right to maintain their specific ancestral legal 'ordinances' rather than being absorbed into a universal legal framework.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad ethnic group within the specific geography of the Peloponnese and their colonies.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from war-related displacement
proof: [§2] And you, son of Deinomenes, the West Locrian girl invokes you, standing outside her door: out of the helpless troubles of war, through your power she looks at the world in security.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights that Hieron's power provides specific security to a person (the Locrian girl) who would otherwise be vulnerable to the 'troubles of war,' implying a rule of protection for subjects/allies under his hegemony.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies specifically to those under the protection of the Syracusean tyranny, particularly vulnerable non-citizens like the Locrians.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold leadership and instruct people
proof: [§1] with your blessing the man who is himself the leader, and who instructs his son, may bring honor to the people and turn them towards harmonious peace.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar describes the passing of leadership and the role of 'instructor' of the people from the ruler to his son, establishing a rule of hereditary political authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to the immediate family of the ruler (the Deinomenids).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be celebrated in communal song
proof: [§1] Phalaris, with his pitiless mind, who burned his victims in a bronze bull, is surrounded on all sides by a hateful reputation; lyres that resound beneath the roof do not welcome him as a theme in gentle partnership with the voices of boys.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar notes that those deemed 'pitiless' are systematically excluded from the communal and religious ritual of the epinician song and lyre-playing, a concrete social resource in Greek life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any ruler who falls into the category of 'hateful reputation' based on specific past actions.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to thrive under any government type
proof: [§2] Under every type of law the man who speaks straightforwardly prospers: in a tyranny, and where the raucous masses oversee the state, and where men of skill do.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that honest or straightforward men are granted the ability to prosper regardless of the constitutional form (tyranny, democracy, or aristocracy).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general rule applied to a large class of 'straightforward' men across different societies.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to speak compellingly among nobles
proof: [§2] A crafty citizen is unable to speak a compelling word among noble men; and yet he fawns on everyone, weaving complete destruction.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'crafty' or slanderous citizens as being barred from effective political or social speech when in the company of the elite (nobles).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific sub-set of citizens characterized by their mode of speech.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to marry without paternal consent
proof: [§3] But she made light of Apollo, in the error of her mind, and consented to another marriage without her father’s knowledge, although she had before lain with Phoebus of the unshorn hair, and was bearing within her the pure seed of the god.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text characterizes Coronis's marriage without her father's knowledge as a grave error and 'lawless deceit' (as mentioned later in the passage), implying that a daughter's marriage required the father's informed consent/authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all unmarried women within the patriarchal family structure of the time.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public wedding ceremonies and choruses
proof: [§3] She did not wait for the marriage-feast to come, nor for the full-voiced cry of the hymenaeal chorus, such things as unmarried girls her own age love to murmur in evening songs to their companion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar identifies the marriage feast and the hymenaeal chorus as standard entitlements or customary expectations for girls of marriageable age, which Coronis forfeited through her clandestine actions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: These rituals were the standard social mechanism for legitimizing marriage for the female population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to resurrect the dead for profit
proof: [§3] But even skill is enthralled by the love of gain. Gold shining in his hand turned even that man, for a handsome price, to bring back from death a man who was already caught. And so the son of Cronus hurled his shaft with his hands through both of them, and swiftly tore the breath out of their chests
extracted_rules.reasoning: The myth of Asclepius serves as a boundary-defining rule: physicians, despite their skill, are prohibited from reversing death, especially for profit. While mythological in origin, it reflects a societal boundary on the limits of 'tékhnē' (skill) and the mortal condition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those possessing the 'skill' of medicine.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to exceptional divine favor and prosperity
proof: [§3] For great destiny watches over the leader of the people, the tyrant, if over any man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar explicitly links the status of the 'tyrant' (ruler) with a higher degree of protection and 'good fortune' compared to the average person, reflecting the political stratification of Sicily in 474 BCE.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The term 'tyrant' refers to a very narrow elite at the top of the political hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to recover ancestral throne
proof: [§4] I have come to my home to recover the ancient honor of my father, now held improperly, which once Zeus granted to Aeolus, the leader of the people, and to his sons. For I hear that lawless Pelias, yielding to his empty mind, violently robbed it from my parents, who were the rulers by right.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Jason's claim to the throne is based on his lineage as the son of the rightful ruler Aeson and descendant of Aeolus. The text framing Pelias's rule as 'improper' and 'robbed' confirms that the legal/customary norm favored the direct male line of the previous king.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically concerns the transition of monarchical power within a specific royal house.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to peaceful division of estate
proof: [§4] May the Fates withdraw if there is any hatred between members of the same family, which blots out reverence. It is not right for us to resort to swords of sharp bronze or spears in dividing the great honors of our ancestors.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a customary rule that disputes over ancestral property and 'honors' (titles/resources) between kinsmen should be settled through negotiation and reverence rather than violence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any family members engaged in inheritance or property disputes within the aristocracy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to ancestral land and possessions
proof: [§4] And truly he, like Atlas, now strains against the weight of the sky, far from his ancestral land and his possessions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The description of Damophilus in exile explicitly links his status to being 'far from' (deprived of) his ancestral land and his possessions, indicating that exile involves a concrete loss of material resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Exile is a specific legal status but affected a significant number of political actors in Greek city-states.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection of professional reputation
proof: [§4] Of the sayings of Homer, take to heart and heed this one: “a noble messenger,” he said, “brings the greatest honor to every business.” Even the Muse is exalted by a correct message.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar cites Homeric tradition to emphasize that a messenger's role carries a specific status or 'honor' that facilitates business and civic communication, implying a protected social standing for those in this role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This apply to a specific professional group when performing their duties.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from labor for masters
proof: [§4] Truly, I often urged the sailors who relieve their masters from toil to guard it; but their minds were forgetful
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines the role of the sailors specifically as those who 'relieve their masters from toil,' establishing a rule of labor subordination where their primary function is to perform the physical work on behalf of a superior group.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a large class of manual laborers/mariners in a maritime society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to pursue any marital union
proof: [§4] And indeed Tityus was hunted down by the swift arrow of Artemis... so that men might desire to touch only the loves that are within their reach.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites the mythological punishment of Tityus as a societal deterrent or rule: men are expected to restrict their marital or sexual pursuits to those 'within their reach' (socially or mortally appropriate), implying a loss of unrestricted choice in partners.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general moral/social rule intended for all men in the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to rule the city of Cyrene
proof: [§5] ...when he came to the house of the descendants of Battus who rule by right; but he was welcomed beside the waters of Castalia, and he flung over your hair the prize of honor for the victorious chariot...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific lineage (the Battiads) as holding the right to rule by hereditary or divine mandate ('rule by right'), establishing an exclusive claim to political sovereignty based on descent.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to a single royal house and its members within the city-state of Cyrene.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection of parents from deprivation of honor
proof: [§6] You keep it on your right hand and uphold the commandment, one of the precepts which they say once in the mountains the son of Philyra enjoined on the powerful son of Peleus, when he was separated from his parents: first of the gods, worship the son of Cronus, the loud-voiced ruler of lightning and thunder; and never deprive your parents of such honor during their allotted lifetime.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar cites a traditional 'commandment' (attributed to Chiron) that mandates children must never deprive their parents of honor, framed as a fundamental societal and religious rule.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all children/offspring within the society regarding their behavior toward parents.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public celebration and honorable return
proof: [§8] And you fell from above on the bodies of four opponents, with grim intent; to them no cheerful homecoming was allotted, as it was to you, at the Pythian festival; nor, when they returned to their mothers, did sweet laughter awaken joy. They slink along the back-streets, away from their enemies, bitten by misfortune.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary lack of 'cheerful homecoming' and social shaming ('slink along the back-streets') for those who lose in sacred games, contrasted with the victor's rights to public processions and joy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any man could be an athlete, this specifically governs the subset of participants who lose in major competitions.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hero-worship and post-mortem offerings
proof: [§5] He was blessed when he dwelled among men, and thereafter a hero worshipped by the people. Apart from him, in front of the houses, are the other sacred kings who took their allotted places in Hades, and somehow below the earth they hear, in their minds, great excellence sprinkled with gentle dew by the outpourings of victory-songs.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records the civic practice of deifying deceased kings (like Aristoteles/Battus) as heroes, granting them a unique legal/religious status that includes public worship and a share in the glory of their descendants' songs.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the tiny group of founding or 'sacred' kings within the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit ancestral merit and reputation
proof: [§8] By nature the genuine spirit of the fathers is conspicuous in the sons. I clearly see Alcmaeon, wielding a dappled serpent on his blazing shield, the first at the gates of Cadmus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text asserts a rule of 'nature' (representing a societal norm of lineage) where the 'spirit' and excellence of the father are transferred to the son, granting the son immediate access to the status and martial expectations of his father.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies primarily to the sons of the aristocracy/warrior class, which is a significant but not universal group.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in public athletic contests
proof: [§9] The women saw your many victories at the seasonal rites of Pallas, and each silently prayed that you could be her dear husband, Telesicrates, or her son; and in the Attic Olympia too, and in the contests of deep-bosomed Mother Earth, and in all your local games.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar describes women as spectators who pray for the victors to be their husbands or sons, rather than as participants in the Panhellenic and local games mentioned (Olympia, Pytho), reflecting their exclusion from competing in these civic institutions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This exclusion applied to the vast majority of the female population in Greek city-states regarding major athletic festivals.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold state governance
proof: [§10] We shall further praise his noble brothers, because they exalt and strengthen the traditional laws of the Thessaliaaans; the good piloting of states, handed from father to son, rests in the hands of noble men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the 'piloting of states' (governance) is a hereditary right or duty restricted to 'noble men,' passing from father to son.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a specific class (nobles) within the broader population of the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Long-term prosperity and stability
proof: [§11] For I have found that those of middle rank in a city flourish with longer prosperity, and I find fault with the lot of tyrannies. I am intent upon common excellences.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar observes that those of moderate wealth/status ('middle rank') enjoy more secure and lasting prosperity compared to the volatile and dangerous nature of absolute power (tyrannies).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This identifies a broad socio-economic segment of the citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to marry a specific high-status bride
proof: [§9] For right away he stood the whole band of suitors at the end of a course, and told them to decide with a footrace which of the heroes, who came to be bridegrooms, would take which bride. The Libyan too made such an offer in joining his daughter with a husband.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary rule where a father forces suitors to compete in a physical trial (a footrace) to determine who is eligible to marry his daughter, effectively denying the resource to all but the winner.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a specific aristocratic marriage custom involving a limited number of elite participants.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete for Pythian garlands
proof: [§10] For he is trying his hand at contests, and the gorge of Parnassus proclaimed him to the people that live around as the greatest of the boys in the double-course footrace.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights a specific category of competition reserved for 'boys,' granting them access to civic recognition and 'garlands' (awards) through organized athletic contests.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific age/gender demographic within the free population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reproductive and sexual autonomy
proof: [§11] Or was she vanquished by another bed and led astray by their nightly sleeping together? This is the most hateful error for young brides, and is impossible to conceal because other people will talk.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an extra-marital affair as a 'hateful error' for young brides, subject to severe social sanction (gossip/shame), indicating a strict rule of marital fidelity and restriction of sexual movement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies generally to newly married women in the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in Pythian games
proof: [§12] receive this victory garland from Pytho in honor of renowned Midas, and receive the victor himself, champion of Hellas in that art which once Pallas Athena discovered
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies Midas of Acragas as a 'champion of Hellas' (Greece) at the Pytho (Pythian) games. In the context of Pindar's time, participation in the Panhellenic games was a concrete right reserved for those of Greek origin, conferring material rewards such as garlands and public honors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad category of Greek citizens capable of traveling to and competing in international festivals, which is a significant but elite portion of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to refuse a forced bridal bed
proof: [§12] and he repaid Polydectes with a deadly wedding-present for the long slavery of his mother and her forced bridal bed
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the 'long slavery' and 'forced bridal bed' of Danae as a grievance that justified the 'deadly' retribution against Polydectes. This indicates that in the author's social framework, the imposition of a forced marriage/sexual union upon a woman of noble lineage (Danae) was viewed as an illegal or transgressive act equivalent to enslavement, implying a rule protecting such women from such unions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all free-born women within the social order.
